title: Think Like the R Runtime
text:
Preface
Most R tutorials teach you to use functions. This book teaches you to think about what R does when it runs them, all the way down to the numbered boxes of memory the machine is moving numbers between.
If you write any programming language for long enough, you will hit moments when it seems to behave strangely. A function in R captures a variable you thought was local. A reactive expression in a Shiny app fires more often than you expected. A target in a pipeline re-runs when nothing in your data has changed. These are not edge cases. They are the language showing you its underlying machinery, and once you can see that machinery, the strangeness resolves into something you can predict.
This book is a course in how that machinery works, built up in five parts.
Part I starts below the language entirely. Before we can say what an R variable is, we need to know what a variable is on the physical machine: what memory actually is, what an address is, what a pointer is, and what the processor is doing billions of times per second. This is the foundation that every later idea rests on. If you have a computer science background, you can skim it. If you do not, it is the most important part of the book, because every apparently magical behaviour in R turns out to be a plain consequence of the ideas in this one chapter.
Part II builds R’s model of names and values on top of that foundation: environments, closures, lazy evaluation, and the call stack. By the end you will understand why targets can plan a pipeline before running it, and why a closure can remember a value long after the function that created it has returned.
Part III is about organising behaviour: R’s object systems (S3, S4, R6) and the reactive programming model that powers Shiny.
Part IV is about speed: why some R code is thousands of times faster than other code that computes the same answer, and how the three layers of caching in the R ecosystem are really one idea applied at three timescales.
Part V is about the deepest idea in the language: that code is itself data, something a program can inspect, build, and run. It starts with metaprogramming in R generally, the toolkit behind formulas and the tidyverse, and culminates in writing a small working interpreter for R, in R. Building a runtime is the surest way to understand one, and it is where every earlier idea in the book comes together at once.
A short capstone then shows every one of these ideas living together inside a single realistic system.
crumbs: 1  Think Like the R Runtime

title: Think Like the R Runtime
text:
0.1 Who this book is for
This book is written for the statistical and machine learning community: people who reach for R to fit models, build reproducible pipelines, and ship interactive apps, and who would like to understand the tool rather than memorise incantations.
Almost no prior R knowledge is assumed. You should be comfortable with the idea of variables, functions, and lists, and you should be numerate, meaning comfortable with abstraction and willing to follow a careful argument. No background in computer architecture is required: Part I builds it from nothing. No JavaScript knowledge is required either, despite the origin of this book’s structure, explained below.
crumbs: 1  Think Like the R Runtime

title: Think Like the R Runtime
text:
0.2 How to read it
Run the code as you go. Each chapter contains short R programs, and almost every one of them is something you can paste into an R session and try. The code blocks are written to be read, not executed automatically, so that the book builds and renders without needing a particular set of packages installed. Where a program produces output, the expected output is shown as comments. Treat any surprise as an invitation to investigate, which is the entire spirit of the book.
Each chapter ends with a short set of exercises and a summary of what you can now predict. The goal is not to finish the book quickly. The goal is to finish a chapter and feel that you can see what was previously invisible.






NoteA note on the code



The R programs in this book are written carefully but are meant to be run and explored by you rather than taken as verified test output. If a result surprises you, run it, read the relevant help page, and reason about it using the model this book gives you. That habit, not the memorisation of any individual result, is what the book is trying to build.
crumbs: 1  Think Like the R Runtime

title: Think Like the R Runtime
text:
0.3 Acknowledgements and lineage
The structure of this book, its selection of topics and the order in which they build, follows Sumit Saha’s course Think Like the JavaScript Engine, published on freeCodeCamp in 2026. That course makes a simple and correct argument: you cannot debug, design, or reason about a language by memorising rules, you have to be able to predict what the runtime will do. This book carries that argument over to R.
The mapping between the two languages is faithful. Where JavaScript has scope, R has lexical environments. Where JavaScript has hoisting, R has lazy evaluation. Where JavaScript has prototypes, R has S3, S4, and R6. Where JavaScript has the event loop, R has the reactive graph. In most cases R’s version of each idea turns out to be the more interesting one, because R was designed by statisticians who wanted a language they could reason about.
None of the text here is taken from that course; only the pedagogical skeleton is shared, with gratitude.
Part V, on code as data, draws its inspiration from a second source: Abelson and Sussman’s Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs (MIT Press), and in particular its idea of teaching a language by building an evaluator for it. The interpreter developed in Chapter 10, and all of the code and prose throughout this book, are original and written for R; what is borrowed from that classic is the pedagogical idea, not its expression. Readers who enjoy Part V should treat SICP as the natural place to go deeper.
The two books that go deeper than this course, and that every serious R programmer should eventually read, are Hadley Wickham’s Advanced R and Norman Matloff’s The Art of R Programming. Where this book ends, those begin.

crumbs: 1  Think Like the R Runtime

title: 2  What the Machine Is Actually Doing
text:
2.1 Everything is switches
Before we can talk about how R organises your variables, we have to talk about what a variable physically is. Not as a metaphor, but as an arrangement of electrical switches in a chip. This chapter builds, from the level of a single switch up to the R interpreter, the substrate that every later idea in this book rests on.
The payoff is concrete. By the end of this chapter you will be able to answer, from first principles, questions that otherwise have to be memorised:
Every one of these has the same kind of answer, and that answer lives here.
At the very bottom, a computer is an enormous collection of switches, each of which is either on or off. We write “on” as 1 and “off” as 0. One such switch is a bit.
A single bit is not much. It can record one yes-or-no fact: true or false, present or absent. But bits combine. Put eight of them in a row and you get a byte, which can take any of \(2^8 = 256\) distinct patterns, from 00000000 to 11111111. If we agree to read those patterns as numbers, a byte can hold any whole number from 0 to 255. Now we can count.
Everything else is built by agreement on top of this. A number larger than 255 does not fit in one byte, so we use several together: four bytes give \(2^{32}\), about 4.3 billion patterns, enough for most counting. Text is bytes too. By a near-universal agreement called ASCII, the letter A is the number 65, which is the byte 01000001. A decimal value like 3.14 is stored using another agreement (a format called IEEE 754) spread across eight bytes.
Here is where intuition usually goes wrong: a byte does not know what it means. The pattern 01000001 is the number 65, and the letter A, and part of some larger number, all at once. Meaning comes entirely from how a program chooses to interpret the pattern. The bytes are mute. The program supplies the interpretation.
crumbs: 2  What the Machine Is Actually Doing

title: 2  What the Machine Is Actually Doing
text:
NoteAnalogy: a wall of light switches



Picture a wall covered in millions of light switches. At any instant the wall is in some configuration of on and off. That configuration is the computer’s current state. Computing is nothing more than rules for flipping switches based on the current pattern of switches. Everything that follows, every number, every model, every Shiny app, is a very elaborate story we tell about which switches are on.
crumbs: 2  What the Machine Is Actually Doing

title: 2  What the Machine Is Actually Doing
section: 2.2 Memory is a street of numbered boxes
text:
2.2 Memory is a street of numbered boxes
Now the single most useful mental model in this book.
Imagine a very long street. Along one side, in order, stands a row of identical small boxes. Each box holds exactly one byte. Each box has a number painted on its front: the first box is box 0, the next is box 1, then box 2, and so on, up into the billions. That painted number is the box’s address.
This street is your computer’s memory, its RAM. That is, almost literally, all that RAM is: a vast, ordered array of byte-sized boxes, each one individually reachable by its address.
When a program wants to store a value, it does two things. It picks one or more boxes, and it writes the bytes of the value into them. To retrieve the value later, it has to know which boxes, which means it has to know the address.
Here is the first deep idea of the book.






ImportantKey idea: a variable is a name in a side-table



The machine has no notion of the name x. What physically exists is a value sitting in some boxes, plus an address that tells you where those boxes are. A “variable” is a human convenience: a label that the programming language keeps in a separate lookup table, mapping the name x to the address where x’s value lives.


Hold onto that, because in the next chapter we will discover that R’s environments are exactly such lookup tables, and that single fact is the whole secret behind how scope works.
Let us make it concrete. Suppose somewhere inside a running R session the following happens.
x <- 10
Underneath, the runtime does roughly this. It finds some free boxes on the street, say starting at address 4096, and writes the bytes representing the number 10 into them. Then, in a side-table for the current scope, it records an entry: the name "x" maps to address 4096. When you later write x + 5, the runtime looks up "x" in the side-table, gets the address 4096, reads the bytes there, interprets them as the number 10, and adds 5.
You never see the address. The whole point of a programming language is to let you say x and not 4096. But the address is what is really there, and several of R’s most confusing behaviours become obvious the moment you remember that names and addresses are two different things.
crumbs: 2  What the Machine Is Actually Doing

title: 2  What the Machine Is Actually Doing
section: 2.3 A pointer is a value that is an address
text:
2.3 A pointer is a value that is an address
Since an address is just a number, and a box can hold a number, a box can hold an address. A box whose contents are the address of another box is called a pointer. We say the first box “points to” the second.






NoteAnalogy: a sticky note with a locker number



A pointer is like a sticky note that reads “the thing you want is in locker 4,815”. The note is small and cheap to carry around and copy. The contents of the locker might be huge. If I hand you a copy of the note, we are now both looking at the same locker. Neither of us has copied what is inside it. We have only copied the note.


This sounds almost too simple to be important. It is, in fact, one of the most powerful ideas in all of computing, and it is the key to three things that confuse nearly every R programmer:

Why y <- x sometimes copies data and sometimes does not. (Chapter 8: it copies the note, not the locker, until someone needs to change the contents.)
Why environments behave unlike every other R object. (Chapter 2: an environment is handled through a pointer that is never silently copied.)
How a single R list can hold a number, a data frame, and a function all at once. (The list does not contain those things; it contains pointers to them, and a pointer is the same small size no matter how large the thing it points to.)

Picture two names, x and y, after y <- x. There is one value on the heap, and two pointers aimed at it.
 name table          memory (the heap)
 ----------          ----------------
   x  ----------\
                 >----->  [ the vector 1, 2, 3, ... ]
   y  ----------/
Copying y <- x did not duplicate the vector. It duplicated the arrow. This picture, and what must happen to it when someone tries to change the vector through y, is the entire content of “copy-on-modify”, which we reach in Chapter 8.
crumbs: 2  What the Machine Is Actually Doing

title: 2  What the Machine Is Actually Doing
section: 2.4 Two neighbourhoods: the stack and the heap
text:
2.4 Two neighbourhoods: the stack and the heap
Programs do not treat memory as one undifferentiated street. They carve it into regions with different rules. Two of those regions matter for everything that follows.
The stack is the region used for the short-lived bookkeeping of function calls. It behaves exactly like a stack of plates: you may add a plate to the top, or remove the plate from the top, and nothing else. When a function is called, the program pushes a fresh plate, called a stack frame, onto the top. That frame holds the call’s local variables and, importantly, the address to jump back to when the function finishes. When the function returns, its frame is popped off the top and instantly forgotten. The stack is fast and fully automatic, but its discipline is strict: a function’s frame vanishes the moment the function returns.
The heap is the region for values that need to outlive the function call that created them, or that are too large, or whose size is not known in advance. Getting a box from the heap takes more work than pushing onto the stack, and something has to decide when a heap box is no longer needed and may be reused. In low-level languages the programmer makes that decision by hand. In R, a part of the runtime called the garbage collector does it automatically, reclaiming any heap memory that nothing points to any more.
This split pays off twice later in the book, so it is worth fixing now.






ImportantKey idea: the stack is temporary, the heap persists



Local bookkeeping for a function call lives on the stack and dies when the call returns. The actual data your program works with lives on the heap and survives as long as something points to it. Closures (Chapter 3) work precisely because R can keep an environment alive on the heap after the stack frame that created it is long gone. The “call stack” you inspect after an error (Chapter 5) is, quite literally, this stack of frames.








NoteAnalogy: scratch paper and a filing cabinet



The stack is a pad of scratch paper. For each task you tear off a fresh sheet, jot down what you need while working, and throw the sheet away the instant the task is done. The heap is a filing cabinet. You put things there when they need to outlast the current task, and a clerk (the garbage collector) periodically removes any file that no sticky note still refers to.
crumbs: 2  What the Machine Is Actually Doing

title: 2  What the Machine Is Actually Doing
section: 2.5 The processor: a fast, literal clerk
text:
2.5 The processor: a fast, literal clerk
So far we have memory, but nothing that acts. The actor is the CPU, the central processing unit.
The CPU does astonishingly little, astonishingly fast. In a tight loop, billions of times per second, it does three things: it fetches the next instruction from memory, decodes what that instruction means, and executes it. Then it does it again.
The instructions it understands are tiny and utterly concrete. There is no instruction for “fit a regression”. The instructions are more like “load the bytes at address A into register 3”, “add register 3 and register 4 and put the result in register 5”, and “if register 5 is zero, jump to the instruction at address B”. Everything your computer does, every model fit and every web page, is a tower of abstractions resting on billions of these minuscule steps.
Registers are the handful, a few dozen, of tiny storage slots physically inside the CPU. They are the only places the CPU can actually do arithmetic. To add two numbers that live in memory, the CPU must first load each into a register, add the registers, and store the result back out to memory. Registers are blindingly fast precisely because there are so few of them and they sit right on the chip.






NoteAnalogy: a clerk with a tiny desk



Imagine an immensely fast clerk working at a tiny desk in a colossal warehouse (memory). The desk has room for only a few items at a time (the registers). To combine two items stored deep in the warehouse, the clerk must walk out, fetch each one to the desk, do the work at the desk, and carry the result back to a shelf. The clerk is so fast that the walking, not the thinking, is what takes the time. Keep that image: in modern computers, moving data is usually the expensive part, not computing on it.
crumbs: 2  What the Machine Is Actually Doing

title: 2  What the Machine Is Actually Doing
section: 2.6 The memory hierarchy, and why nearness is speed
text:
2.6 The memory hierarchy, and why nearness is speed
That last analogy points at the most important performance fact in modern computing. There is a cruel trade-off in hardware: memory that is fast is also small and expensive, while memory that is large is slow and cheap. No single kind of memory is both big and fast. So computers stack several kinds into a hierarchy, fastest and smallest at the top:

Registers: a few dozen slots, inside the CPU, access in well under a nanosecond.
Cache (levels L1, L2, L3): kilobytes to a few megabytes, very near the CPU, a few nanoseconds.
Main memory (RAM): gigabytes, around 100 nanoseconds, which is roughly a hundred times slower than cache.
Disk or SSD: terabytes, microseconds to milliseconds, dramatically slower again.

When the CPU needs a byte, it first checks the nearest cache. If the byte is there (a cache hit), wonderful. If not (a cache miss), it must go to a slower level, which can be a hundred times the wait. And here is the detail that drives a great deal of real-world performance: when the CPU fetches one byte from RAM, it does not fetch just that byte. It grabs a whole neighbouring chunk, called a cache line, typically 64 bytes, on the bet that you will very soon want the bytes sitting right next door.
That bet is the physical reason that walking through data laid out contiguously in memory is far faster than chasing pointers scattered across the heap. With contiguous data, every expensive trip to RAM brings back 64 useful bytes at once. With scattered pointers, each trip may bring back 63 bytes you do not want. We bank this insight now and cash it in Chapter 8, where it explains why R’s vectorised operations are fast and its pointer-chasing patterns are slow.






ImportantKey idea: locality is speed



Data that sits together is processed together, quickly. Data that is scattered forces the processor to wait on memory again and again. A surprising amount of “make it faster” in numerical computing is really “make the data contiguous so the cache bet pays off”.
crumbs: 2  What the Machine Is Actually Doing

title: 2  What the Machine Is Actually Doing
section: 2.7 What R adds: an interpreter is just a program
text:
2.7 What R adds: an interpreter is just a program
We now have enough to say precisely what R is.
R is a program, written mostly in the C language, that runs on the machine we have just described. When you type R code, you are not speaking to the CPU directly. You are handing text to the R interpreter, a C program that reads your code and, on your behalf, performs the corresponding manipulations of memory: allocating boxes on the heap, writing values into them, recording names and addresses in side-tables, and orchestrating the CPU to do the arithmetic.
Every R value you create is, underneath, a C structure living on the heap. R’s internals call these structures SEXPs (short for S-expressions). A SEXP carries a small header (what type is this: an integer vector? a list? a function? and some bookkeeping, including a count of how many things point to it, which we will need in Chapter 8) followed by either the data itself or a pointer to it.
An R environment, the protagonist of the next chapter, is one particular kind of SEXP. In essence it is a hash table mapping names to the SEXPs they are bound to, together with a pointer to a parent environment. Read that sentence again, because once you believe it literally, the entire behaviour of scope, closures, and reference semantics stops being a list of rules and becomes a set of consequences you can derive on your own.






ImportantKey idea: there is no magic



Everything in the rest of this book is a consequence of a C program managing numbered boxes of memory for you. When R surprises you, the explanation is never magic. It is always a fact about names, addresses, pointers, the stack, the heap, or the cache, and you now know all six.
crumbs: 2  What the Machine Is Actually Doing

title: 2  What the Machine Is Actually Doing
text:
2.8 What you can now predict
You have not written any “real” R yet, but you can already reason about it. With only the ideas in this chapter, you should be able to explain, at least in outline:

Why two variables can refer to the same underlying data, and what copying really copies.
Why some things a function creates disappear when it returns, while others survive.
Why the layout of your data in memory can matter as much as the algorithm.
Why an interpreted language like R can be slow in a loop yet fast on a whole vector at once.

Each of these becomes a precise, mechanical story in the chapters ahead. We begin with the side-table that maps names to values: the environment.
crumbs: 2  What the Machine Is Actually Doing

title: 2  What the Machine Is Actually Doing
text:
2.9 Exercises

In your own words, explain the difference between a name, an address, and a value. Use the locker analogy, then restate it without any analogy at all.
Suppose a is a vector of one million numbers and you write b <- a. Before reading Chapter 8, predict what is copied at that moment: the million numbers, or something much smaller? Justify your answer using only the pointer picture from this chapter.
A function creates a local counter, increments it, and returns. Using the stack-and-heap distinction, explain why that counter would normally be gone after the function returns, and sketch what would have to be true for it to survive. (You are predicting the mechanism behind closures, which is Chapter 3.)
Explain, using the cache-line idea, why summing the elements of one contiguous vector of a million numbers can be much faster than summing a million numbers each stored in a separate object linked by pointers, even though the count of additions is identical.
crumbs: 2  What the Machine Is Actually Doing

title: 3  Where Variables Live
text:
3.1 An environment is a side-table with a parent
In the foundations chapter (Chapter 1) we established that a variable is a name in a side-table, mapping a string to an address on the heap. R has a specific name for that side-table. It calls it an environment, and it makes environments into real, inspectable objects. This chapter is about what they are and how lookups walk between them. Almost everything later in the book is a consequence of this one mechanism.
Recall the picture from the foundations chapter: when you write x <- 10, the runtime puts the value 10 in some boxes on the heap and records, in a side-table, that the name "x" maps to that location. An environment is that side-table. Concretely it is a hash table from names to values (technically to the SEXPs those names are bound to), plus one extra pointer: a link to a parent environment.
That parent pointer is the entire trick. When R needs the value of a name and does not find it in the current environment, it follows the parent pointer and looks in the parent, then the parent’s parent, and so on. The search stops when the name is found, or when R reaches the topmost environment (the empty environment) and gives up with an error.
So an environment is two things bolted together: a table of name-to-value bindings, and a pointer to where to look next. Keep that shape in mind. We will see it reused, almost unchanged, as the basis of closures, of R6 objects, and of Shiny’s reactive bookkeeping.

title: 3  Where Variables Live
text:
x <- 10

environment()         # the environment you are typing into,
                      # known as the global environment
ls(environment())     # list every name bound in it
get("x", envir = environment())   # retrieve the value bound to "x"

title: 3  Where Variables Live
section: 3.2 Why one environment is not enough
text:
3.2 Why one environment is not enough
A useful R session quickly contains many environments, not one. The reason is functions, and it follows directly from the stack-and-heap distinction in the foundations chapter (Chapter 1).
Consider defining and calling a function.
f <- function(x) {
  y <- x * 2
  y + 1
}

f(3)    # 7
When R runs f(3), it needs somewhere to keep the local names x (bound to 3) and y (bound to 6). Those names must not collide with any x or y you happen to have in the console. So R creates a fresh environment for this one call, the call’s stack frame from the foundations chapter (Chapter 1), and binds the local names there. The environment lives for the duration of the call and is normally discarded when f returns.
But functions also mention names that are not their own arguments.
scaling_factor <- 0.5

scale <- function(x) x * scaling_factor

scale(10)    # 5
When scale(10) runs, R cannot find scaling_factor in the function’s own call environment. So it follows the parent pointer and looks in a wider environment. This is the second idea the chapter turns on: every environment has a parent, and name lookup walks the chain of parents until the name is found or the chain runs out.

title: 3  Where Variables Live
text:
3.3 The search path
Here is the chain that exists during a typical session. Each entry is an environment, and the parent pointers run down the list.
search()
# [1] ".GlobalEnv"        "package:stats"
# [3] "package:graphics"  "package:grDevices"
# [5] "package:utils"     "package:datasets"
# [7] "package:methods"   "Autoloads"
# [9] "package:base"
The chain runs from the global environment, where you type, down to the base environment, where built-in functions like sum and length live. When you reference a name, R walks this chain in order. This also explains an occasional source of confusion: if two packages both export a function called select, the one you loaded more recently wins, because library() inserts each new package near the front of the chain, ahead of the older one.






NoteAnalogy: a chain of dictionaries



Think of each environment as a dictionary, and the parent pointer as a note at the back saying “if you did not find your word here, try this other dictionary next”. To look up a word you start in your own dictionary and keep following the notes outward until you find a definition or run out of dictionaries.

title: 3  Where Variables Live
section: 3.4 Lexical scoping: where a function looks
text:
3.4 Lexical scoping: where a function looks
One subtle question remains, and it is the heart of the chapter. When scale needs scaling_factor, whose parent chain does it walk? There are two candidates. It could walk the chain of the environment where the function was defined, or the chain of the environment it was called from.
R uses the first. This rule is called lexical scoping: every function carries a pointer to the environment in which it was created, and free names (names that are not its arguments) are looked up starting there. The alternative, resolving names from wherever the function happens to be called, is called dynamic scoping, and R rejects it, as do almost all modern languages, because it would make a function’s meaning depend on its caller, and reasoning would collapse.
The cleanest demonstration is a function that captures a value that is later changed.
x <- "global"

make_printer <- function() {
  x <- "local to make_printer"
  function() print(x)        # this inner function is
                              # defined inside make_printer
}

p <- make_printer()
x <- "changed in global"

p()
# [1] "local to make_printer"
# Not "changed in global", and not "global".
Read that slowly. The inner function does not even exist when x <- "global" runs. By the time the inner function is finally called, the line x <- "changed in global" has executed. Yet the inner function prints the value of x that was in scope where it was defined. The function held a pointer to its definition environment, and, exactly as the foundations chapter (Chapter 1) predicted, that pointer kept the environment alive on the heap long after make_printer returned and its stack frame was gone. The next chapter, on closures, is entirely about this.






ImportantKey idea: a function is body plus environment



A function’s behaviour depends on three things: its arguments, its body, and the environment in which it was defined. Two functions with identical arguments and identical bodies can produce different results because their definition environments differ. This is the single most important fact about R functions, and it is a direct consequence of lexical scoping.

title: 3  Where Variables Live
3  Where Variables Live
section: 3.6 Environments are the one exception to copy-on-modify
text:
3.6 Environments are the one exception to copy-on-modify
That last line points at something important, and it reaches straight back to pointers in the foundations chapter (Chapter 1). Almost every object in R is handled with copy-on-modify semantics, which we study properly in Chapter 8: if two names point to the same value and you modify it through one of them, R quietly makes a copy first so the other name is unaffected.
Environments are the single deliberate exception. They are passed and assigned by reference, meaning the pointer is shared and never silently copied. If you pass an environment to a function and the function modifies it, the change is visible everywhere, because every name involved is pointing at the very same table on the heap.
e <- new.env()
e$value <- 1

bump <- function(env) env$value <- env$value + 1

bump(e)
e$value    # 2, the change is visible outside the function
This is not a quirk. It is the property that makes environments useful as the storage behind mutable objects. When you want state that several functions can share and change, an environment is the tool, precisely because it is the one thing R will not copy behind your back.

title: 3  Where Variables Live
section: 3.7 Why this matters for your work
text:
3.7 Why this matters for your work

3.7.1 For a targets pipeline
Every tar_target(name, command) wraps command as a function, and that function carries a definition environment, usually the environment in which your _targets.R file was sourced. If you accidentally leave a large object in that environment (a multi-gigabyte data frame loaded while testing, say), targets that close over it can treat it as part of their dependency footprint and may serialise it to disk. The fix is structural and follows directly from lexical scoping: define your target functions in a separate R/ directory, sourced into a clean environment, so that the definition environment contains only your helper functions and none of your scratch data.


3.7.2 For a Shiny app
The server function runs once per user session. Every reactive(), observe(), and output$... assignment inside it closes over that session’s server environment. This is why a plain x <- 0 at the top of server acts as per-session state, private to one user, while an object created outside server, in global.R, is shared across all sessions: each session’s closures walk their parent chain up into the same outer environment and find the one shared copy there. Scope, in other words, is your main tool for deciding what is per-user and what is global.

title: 3  Where Variables Live
text:
3.8 What you can now predict

Why a function can see variables from where it was written, but not from where it is called.
Why moving a heavy computation outside a Shiny server function shares it across users, while leaving it inside isolates it per user.
Why environments, alone among R objects, let a change made in one place be seen in another.
Why a pipeline tool can accidentally drag a large object into a target, and how scope discipline prevents it.

title: 3  Where Variables Live
text:
3.9 Exercises

Without running it, predict what each of the three cat() calls below prints. Then run it and explain any surprise using the side-table picture from this chapter.
x <- 1
f <- function() {
  cat(x, "\n")
  x <- 2
  cat(x, "\n")
}
f()
cat(x, "\n")
Use new.env(), assign(), and get() to build a tiny key-value store. Make it reject re-assignment to a key that already exists. You will not need any package.
Write parent_chain(env), returning a list of all ancestor environments of env, ending at the empty environment. Test it on the global environment, then run library(dplyr) and test again. What changed in the chain, and where?
Demonstrate the reference behaviour of environments. Create an environment, pass it to two different functions that each modify it, and confirm that both changes are visible afterward. Then do the “same” thing with a list and explain why the list does not behave the same way.

title: 4  Closures
text:
4.1 The three parts of a function
Every function in R is a closure. Most R programmers do not know this, and the ones who do are usually the ones writing the libraries everyone else depends on. This chapter explains what that sentence means, why it follows directly from the previous two chapters, and how it quietly powers function factories, the cache in memoise, R6 objects, and Shiny reactives.
We can now state precisely what an R function is. A function is exactly three things: its arguments (the names between the parentheses), its body (the expression to evaluate), and its definition environment (the environment in which it was created, introduced in the previous chapter). R lets you pull out all three.
When you call f(3), four things happen, and they are exactly the heap-and-stack mechanics from Chapter 1. R creates a new environment whose parent is environment(f). It binds x = 3 and y = 2 into that environment. It evaluates the body there. Then it returns the result and discards the environment, unless something else still points to it. That italicised clause is the whole definition of a closure.

title: 4  Closures
text:
f <- function(x, y = 2) x + y

formals(f)         # the arguments: x, and y with default 2
body(f)            # the body: x + y
environment(f)     # the definition environment







ImportantKey idea: a closure is a function plus a captured environment



A closure is a function together with a reference to its definition environment that survives the call which created it. Because every R function carries a pointer to its definition environment, every R function is technically a closure. The idea only becomes interesting when that captured environment holds state the function can read or change.

title: 4  Closures
text:
4.2 The factory pattern
The clearest demonstration of closures is a function that returns a function.
make_adder <- function(n) {
  function(x) x + n
}

add3 <- make_adder(3)
add5 <- make_adder(5)

add3(10)    # 13
add5(10)    # 15

environment(add3)$n    # 3
environment(add5)$n    # 5
Trace it through the Chapter 1 picture. We called make_adder twice. Each call created a fresh environment on the heap containing a binding for n. Each call returned an inner function whose definition environment is that fresh environment. The two returned functions therefore have identical arguments (x) and identical bodies (x + n), yet they behave differently, because they carry different definition environments. The last two lines prove it by reaching directly into each closure’s captured environment and reading the n it kept.
This pattern is everywhere in the R ecosystem once you start looking. The scales package gives you label_dollar(prefix = "$"), which returns a formatting function. The purrr package gives you partial(f, x = 1), which returns a function with one argument pinned. Many statistical constructions are naturally families of functions: a smoothing routine that returns a fitted function, a likelihood builder that returns a function of the parameters. Whenever the right design is “a configurable family of functions”, the closure-based factory is usually the cleanest way to express it.






NoteAnalogy: a cookie cutter with a setting



make_adder is a cookie cutter with a dial. You set the dial to 3, stamp out a function, and that function “remembers” the 3 forever because the setting was baked into its own private environment at the moment of stamping. Set the dial to 5 and stamp again, and you get a different function with a different memory. The cutter (the factory) and the cookies (the returned closures) are different things.

title: 4  Closures
section: 4.3 Hidden state and the superassignment arrow

title: 4  Closures
section: 4.4 The classic trap: closures in a loop
text:
4.4 The classic trap: closures in a loop
The single most common closure bug in R involves capturing a loop variable. The example is small, but the same shape appears in larger Shiny apps and package code, so it is worth burning into memory.
fs <- list()
for (i in 1:3) {
  fs[[i]] <- function() i
}

fs[[1]]()    # 3, not 1
fs[[2]]()    # 3, not 2
fs[[3]]()    # 3
Why does every stored function return 3? Because all three inner functions close over the same environment, the one the for loop ran in. There is only one i in that environment. By the time you call the stored functions, the loop has finished and that single i holds its final value, 3. The closures faithfully look up i and report what they find. Nothing is broken; the functions are doing exactly what lexical scoping says they must.
The fix is to give each function its own environment with its own i. The standard way is to use a function that creates a fresh call environment per iteration.
fs <- lapply(1:3, function(i) {
  function() i
})

fs[[1]]()    # 1
fs[[2]]()    # 2
fs[[3]]()    # 3
Now each iteration is a separate call to the function passed to lapply, and each call makes its own environment with its own i. The functions returned from those calls close over different environments and report different values. This is also exactly why purrr::map is immune to the bug: it uses the same per-element call mechanism.

title: 4  Closures
4  Closures
section: 4.6 Why this matters for your work
text:
4.6 Why this matters for your work
Once the factory pattern is clear, you will start to see it in your own code, and reaching for it will make that code simpler. When you find yourself writing several near-identical functions that differ only in one or two settings, the right design is almost always a single factory that captures the settings in a closure and returns a clean function that never mentions them. This shows up when building a family of loss functions for an optimiser, a set of log-likelihoods over different priors, or a collection of themed plotting or table-formatting helpers for a report. The configuration goes into the closure; the interface stays simple.

title: 4  Closures
text:
4.7 What you can now predict

Why two functions with the same body can behave differently.
Why a counter built from a closure keeps its own private, persistent state.
Why all the functions made in a naive loop end up sharing one value, and how to fix it.
Why caching, reactivity, and pipeline tools are, underneath, the same closure-plus-table idea.

title: 4  Closures
text:
4.8 Exercises

Modify make_counter() to accept a by argument so the step size is configurable, and add a history() method that returns every value the counter has ever held.
Write throttle(f, n), returning a wrapped version of f that actually calls f only on every n-th invocation and returns NULL the rest of the time. Use only closures, no extra packages.
Extend the eight-line memoise so that cache entries expire after a fixed wall-clock duration. Where, physically, does the expiry time for each entry live? What goes in the cache environment now, and what stays in the closure?
Predict the output of the loop trap example before running it, then fix it two different ways: once with lapply, and once by keeping the for loop but forcing a fresh binding per iteration with local(). Explain, in terms of environments, why each fix works.

title: 5  Lazy Evaluation and Promises
text:
5.1 A surprising experiment
In R, function arguments are not evaluated when you pass them. They are wrapped in a small object, parked, and evaluated later, only if and when they are actually used. This is the single deepest feature separating R from almost every mainstream language, and it is the mechanism that lets dplyr understand filter(df, x > 3) and lets targets plan a pipeline before running a line of it.
Start with a function whose argument announces when it is evaluated.
Read the output carefully. entered f prints before either argument is evaluated, even though noisy("A") and noisy("B") are written right there in the call. Each argument is evaluated only at the moment its name is first used inside the body. That is lazy evaluation, and once you have seen it you cannot unsee it.

title: 5  Lazy Evaluation and Promises
text:
noisy <- function(label) {
  cat("evaluating", label, "\n")
  label
}

f <- function(a, b) {
  cat("entered f\n")
  cat("about to use a\n")
  a
  cat("about to use b\n")
  b
}

f(noisy("A"), noisy("B"))
# entered f
# about to use a
# evaluating A
# about to use b
# evaluating B
# [1] "B"

title: 5  Lazy Evaluation and Promises
text:
5.2 The mechanism: promises
When R prepares to call a function, it does not evaluate the arguments and then enter the function. Instead, for each argument, it creates a small object called a promise. Building on Chapter 1, a promise is a structure on the heap holding three things: the original unevaluated expression, a pointer to the environment where that expression should eventually be evaluated (the calling environment), and an empty slot for the resulting value.
When the body first uses the argument by name, the promise is forced: its expression is evaluated in the stored environment, the result is written into the value slot, and from then on the promise behaves like a plain value. Forcing happens at most once. Ask for the argument ten times and the expression runs once; the other nine reads return the cached value.






NoteAnalogy: an IOU



A promise is an IOU. When you call a function, you do not hand it the goods, you hand it a note describing where to get them and the address to fetch them from. The function only redeems the note if it actually needs the goods. If it never looks, the goods are never fetched, which is why an unused argument that would have thrown an error often causes no error at all.

title: 5  Lazy Evaluation and Promises
text:
5.3 Consequences you can rely on

5.3.1 Default arguments can refer to other arguments
Because defaults are themselves promises, evaluated inside the function’s own environment when first used, a default can depend on another argument.
f <- function(x, y = x * 2) x + y

f(3)        # 9, because y defaults to x * 2 = 6
f(3, 10)    # 13, because y is supplied
The default x * 2 is not computed when the function is defined. It is a parked expression that is evaluated, if needed, inside the call, where x is in scope.


5.3.2 You can capture the expression, not just the value
R lets you reach into a promise and pull out the unevaluated expression. The built-in primitive is substitute().
inspect <- function(x) {
  expr <- substitute(x)               # the unevaluated expression
  cat("Expression:", deparse(expr), "\n")
  cat("Value:     ", eval(expr), "\n")
}

a <- 5
inspect(a + 7)
# Expression: a + 7
# Value:      12
This is the foundation of what R calls non-standard evaluation. When dplyr::filter(df, x > 3) works even though there is no x defined in your console, it works because filter grabs the unevaluated expression x > 3 from its promise, looks inside it, and evaluates it in the data frame’s environment, where x is the name of a column, instead of in your console’s environment. The promise carried both the expression and a choice of where to run it, and filter overrode the where.

title: 5  Lazy Evaluation and Promises
5  Lazy Evaluation and Promises
text:
5.5 How targets builds its graph
This is the chapter where targets stops being magic. Consider a typical pipeline definition.
list(
  tar_target(raw,   read_csv("data.csv")),
  tar_target(clean, clean_fn(raw)),
  tar_target(model, fit(clean))
)
The second argument of each tar_target() is a promise. targets does not evaluate these promises when you call tar_target(). Instead it captures the unevaluated expressions, the same move as substitute(), and walks each expression with codetools::findGlobals() to discover which other names it mentions. From those references it builds a directed graph. The edges raw -> clean and clean -> model are inferred at this stage, purely by parsing unevaluated code. No data has been read and no model has been fitted.
Only when you later run tar_make() are the promises forced, and even then only in dependency order, and only for targets whose stored hash no longer matches. Every part of this story rests on lazy evaluation. Without it, the pipeline would have to run in order to be planned, which would defeat the entire purpose of planning.






ImportantKey idea: code as data



Lazy evaluation lets R treat a piece of code as data before deciding whether, where, or even whether at all to run it. dplyr inspects the code to choose where to evaluate it. targets inspects the code to build a graph and decide what to skip. This ability to look at code before running it is what people mean when they call R a language well suited to metaprogramming.

title: 5  Lazy Evaluation and Promises
text:
5.6 Why this matters for Shiny
A reactive() expression is, mechanically, a stored promise that can be forced more than once. The block of code you pass to reactive() is captured unevaluated; it is forced only when something downstream asks for its value, and it is re-forced when its inputs have changed. The isolate() function is a controlled forcing: it evaluates a reactive’s expression without registering a dependency, so the surrounding reactive context does not get told to update when that value later changes. Chapter 7 takes this apart in detail. For now, notice that “a parked computation, evaluated on demand” is the same idea you met as memoise in Chapter 3 and will meet again as the reactive graph.

title: 5  Lazy Evaluation and Promises
text:
5.7 What you can now predict

Why an argument that looks like it should error sometimes causes no error, if the function never uses it.
Why a default argument can be written in terms of another argument.
Why dplyr and friends let you name columns without quotes, and what would break without lazy evaluation.
Why targets can draw and prune a dependency graph before running any of the work.

title: 5  Lazy Evaluation and Promises
text:
5.8 Exercises

Write describe_call(x) that returns a string containing both the unevaluated expression the caller passed and its evaluated value. Use substitute(), deparse(), and eval(). Test it with describe_call(mean(1:10)).
Build a tiny version of targets. Write graph(...) that accepts named expressions like graph(a = 1 + 1, b = a * 2), builds a dependency graph by inspecting the unevaluated expressions, and returns the targets in dependency order. Hint: substitute() on the ... and codetools::findGlobals() on each expression.
Explain, in terms of promises and the environment each promise carries, why this prints 3 and not 1.
x <- 1
f <- function(y = x) { x <- 3; y }
f()
Write a function that takes an argument but never uses it, and pass it stop("boom"). Explain why no error occurs. Then add a single line to the function that makes the error appear, and explain precisely what that line did to the promise.

title: 6  The Call Stack
text:
6.1 Two notions of parent
In Chapter 1 we met the stack: a region of memory that grows by a frame when a function is called and shrinks when it returns. R exposes that stack to you. You can read the chain of in-flight calls, inspect the local variables of any frame, and intercept errors and warnings as they travel up it. This chapter is about that machinery and the surprisingly powerful condition system built on top of it.
We have to separate two things the earlier chapters quietly let blur together. Every running function has two relevant environments, and confusing them causes a whole category of advanced R bugs.
The definition environment, also called the lexical parent, is where the function was created. Closures capture this. It is what governs how free names are resolved, by the rules of Chapter 2. You reach it with environment(f).
The calling environment, also called the parent frame, is the environment that contained the expression that invoked the function. It is a fact about this particular call, not about the function. You reach it with parent.frame().
Most ordinary code only ever needs the definition environment, because lexical scoping is what resolves names. The calling environment is the tool you reach for when writing infrastructure: code whose job is to inspect or act on its caller. Error handlers, debugging utilities, and the non-standard-evaluation helpers inside dplyr all use it. If you are not writing that kind of code, you can mostly forget parent.frame() exists, but knowing the distinction is what lets you read other people’s infrastructure code.

title: 6  The Call Stack
6  The Call Stack
text:
6.2 Inspecting the stack at runtime
When a function calls another, which calls another, R keeps a stack of all the in-flight calls, exactly the stack of frames from Chapter 1. You can read it at any moment.
deep <- function() {
  print(sys.calls())     # every call currently on the stack
  print(sys.frames())    # the matching environments
}

a <- function() b()
b <- function() c()
c <- function() deep()

a()
# [[1]] a()
# [[2]] b()
# [[3]] c()
# [[4]] deep()
This is the same machinery that powers traceback() after an error, debug() for stepping into a function, and browser() for pausing execution to inspect any frame’s local variables. The call stack is not a sealed box. It is a list you can walk, which is why R’s debugging tools can be written in R itself.






ImportantKey idea: the stack is data, so the tools are just code



Because the call stack is an ordinary list you can read while a program runs, everything built on top of it is ordinary R reading that list. traceback, browser, and the entire condition system below are not privileged machinery baked into the language; they are programs that walk the stack. A runtime that lets you see its own bookkeeping is a runtime you can extend in its own terms, which is a theme we will take to its conclusion when we build an evaluator in Chapter 10.

title: 6  The Call Stack
6  The Call Stack
section: 6.4 Why R has no tail-call optimisation
text:
6.4 Why R has no tail-call optimisation
A note for readers coming from a functional-programming background. R does not optimise tail calls. A function that recurses very deeply will exhaust the C stack and stop with Error: C stack usage is too close to the limit.
There are two reasons, and both connect to earlier chapters. First, lazy evaluation (Chapter 4) muddies the very notion of a tail call: a call that looks like it is in tail position may still have to force pending promises after it returns, so the frame cannot simply be discarded. Second, R is built to let you introspect its own stack at any point, as we did above, and quietly folding away frames to save space would break that introspection.
The practical advice is short. Do not use recursion for deep iteration in R. Use Reduce(), purrr::reduce(), or a plain loop, all of which run in constant stack space. Recursion is still fine for genuinely tree-shaped problems of bounded depth, such as walking a parse tree or a small nested list, where the depth is small and the recursive shape mirrors the data.

title: 6  The Call Stack
text:
6.5 What you can now predict

Why a function resolves its variable names by where it was written, but can still discover where it was called from when it needs to.
Why R can offer rich debugging tools written in R itself.
Why a well-built pipeline can survive a failure in one stage, and which handler makes that possible.
Why deep recursion blows up in R when it would be fine in some other languages.

title: 6  The Call Stack
text:
6.6 Exercises

Write caller_says(), a function that, when called from inside another function, deparses and prints the expression the caller’s caller wrote when invoking the caller. You will need sys.call(-1).
Using withCallingHandlers(), write with_logging(f), a wrapper that appends every warning emitted by f to a log file but lets f run to completion normally.
Read the help pages for tryCatch() and withCallingHandlers() alongside this chapter, then write one paragraph, in your own words, on the difference between unwinding and calling handlers, with one example where you would actively prefer each.
Write a recursive factorial and call it with a large enough argument to hit the stack limit. Then rewrite it with Reduce() and confirm the limit is gone. Explain, using Chapter 1, what changed about the memory the two versions use.

title: 7  Object Systems: S3, S4, R6
text:
7.1 S3: the simplest possible object system
R has not one object-oriented system but four. Two of them are functional, dispatching on the class of an argument. One is fully encapsulated and, as you will see, is built directly out of the environments from Chapter 2. This chapter explains each, shows how dispatch actually works as a mechanical lookup, and gives you a rule for choosing.
An S3 object is any R value with a class attribute. There are no class declarations, no inheritance contracts, no validation built in. The whole system is a convention enforced by one primitive, UseMethod(), which performs the dispatch.
The dispatch is entirely mechanical, and worth spelling out because it demystifies a great deal of base R. When you call area(x), R enters the body of area, sees UseMethod("area"), reads the class attribute of x, builds the candidate method name area.circle, and looks that name up using the ordinary scoping rules from Chapter 2. If the class attribute lists several classes, R tries them in order. If none match, it falls back to area.default. There is no registry and no magic; method dispatch is just name construction followed by a normal variable lookup.
Most of base R works this way. print, summary, plot, predict, coef, and residuals are all S3 generics. When you fit a linear model with lm(), the result is a list with class "lm", and calling summary(fit) dispatches to summary.lm(). You can write your own summary.my_class() for your own objects and it will be found automatically, because dispatch is just a lookup and your method is just a name in scope.
crumbs: 7  Object Systems: S3, S4, R6

title: 7  Object Systems: S3, S4, R6
text:
# Constructors: just lists carrying a class attribute.
new_circle <- function(radius) {
  structure(list(radius = radius), class = "circle")
}

new_rectangle <- function(width, height) {
  structure(list(width = width, height = height), class = "rectangle")
}

# A generic function: it promises to dispatch on the class of its argument.
area <- function(shape) UseMethod("area")

# Methods, named generic.class.
area.circle    <- function(shape) pi * shape$radius^2
area.rectangle <- function(shape) shape$width * shape$height
area.default   <- function(shape) stop("No area method for class ", class(shape))

area(new_circle(5))           # 78.54
area(new_rectangle(3, 4))     # 12








NoteAnalogy: surnames and a phone book



A generic is like calling out a first name, “area!”, in a crowded room. UseMethod looks at the object’s class to get a surname, “circle”, and pages the specific person “area.circle” out of the phone book (the scope chain). If nobody by that surname answers, it pages the family default.
crumbs: 7  Object Systems: S3, S4, R6

title: 7  Object Systems: S3, S4, R6
section: 7.2 S4: classes that take themselves seriously
text:
7.2 S4: classes that take themselves seriously
S4 is what S3 would look like if the designers had given it formal structure. Classes have named, typed slots. Generics are declared explicitly. Methods are registered with full type signatures, and dispatch can consider more than one argument. The price is verbosity.
setClass("Circle",    representation(radius = "numeric"))
setClass("Rectangle", representation(width = "numeric", height = "numeric"))

setGeneric("area", function(shape) standardGeneric("area"))

setMethod("area", "Circle",    function(shape) pi * shape@radius^2)
setMethod("area", "Rectangle", function(shape) shape@width * shape@height)

area(new("Circle", radius = 5))
Reach for S4 when you are writing a Bioconductor package, where the entire ecosystem is S4 and conforming is the path of least resistance; when you genuinely need multiple dispatch, a method whose behaviour depends on the classes of two or more arguments at once; or when you want slot-level type checking enforced for you. For most everyday work, S3 is enough.
crumbs: 7  Object Systems: S3, S4, R6

title: 7  Object Systems: S3, S4, R6
section: 7.3 R6: encapsulated, mutable, and built from environments
crumbs: 7  Object Systems: S3, S4, R6

title: 7  Object Systems: S3, S4, R6
text:
7.4 How to choose
The decision is structural, not a matter of taste. Ask one question: do I need mutable state shared across method calls?
If your object is essentially data, a fitted model, a configured plot, a tidy result, use S3. Wrap a list in a class, write methods for the generics that matter (print, summary, predict), and stop. This is the right answer the large majority of the time in statistical work.
If you need formal typed slots or multiple dispatch, or you are in the Bioconductor world, use S4.
If you need an object that remembers things between calls and is naturally about managing something over time, a database connection, a streaming file reader, a stateful API client, a cache with explicit invalidation, use R6. The encapsulation that S3 lacks is exactly what you want when an object’s job is to own the lifecycle of an external resource.
crumbs: 7  Object Systems: S3, S4, R6

title: 7  Object Systems: S3, S4, R6
section: 7.5 Why this matters for statistical package design
text:
7.5 Why this matters for statistical package design
For a typical statistical or official-statistics package, estimating small-area means, producing indicators, running scenarios, S3 is almost always right. Your objects are estimated quantities: they hold numbers and metadata, not behaviour. Write a constructor that validates its inputs, a compact set of S3 methods so your objects print and summarise and plot sensibly, and resist the urge to reach for a heavier system. This echoes the advice in Hadley Wickham’s Advanced R: prefer functional design when the domain is mathematical, and the domain of statistics is mathematical.
R6 earns its place when you are managing a resource rather than representing a quantity: a session against a statistical office’s API that authenticates once and is reused, a reader that must remember its position in a large fixed-width file, a connection pool. There the reference semantics and explicit lifecycle are a feature, not overhead.
crumbs: 7  Object Systems: S3, S4, R6

title: 7  Object Systems: S3, S4, R6
text:
7.6 What you can now predict

Why writing summary.myclass is all it takes to make summary() work on your objects.
Why an R6 object handed to two functions lets each see the other’s changes, while an S3 list would not.
Why R6 and the closure-based counter are, underneath, the same mechanism.
Which object system a new piece of code should use, from a single question about state.

text:
7.7 Exercises

Build an S3 class nuts_estimate wrapping a regional estimate: a mean, a standard error, a sample size, and a region code. Implement print, summary, and a plot method that draws a horizontal bar with the confidence interval.
Convert the make_counter() factory from Chapter 3 into an R6 class. Note exactly where you used <<- in the closure version and what replaces it in R6, and explain why the replacement works.
Read the source of shiny::reactiveValues. Identify which object system it uses and explain, in two sentences and with reference to Chapter 2, why that choice is the right one.
