title: 8  Reactivity
text:
8.1 The problem reactivity solves
A Shiny app feels alive: change a slider and a plot redraws, and you never wrote the code that connected them. That feeling is produced by the reactive graph, a network of dependencies through which invalidation signals flow. It is the R counterpart of the event loop in a browser, and it is built from the closures and lazy evaluation you already understand. Once you can see the graph, Shiny stops being mysterious and starts being predictable.
Imagine wiring a small app by hand. Two numeric inputs, one output showing their sum. You could write: when input A changes, recompute the sum; when input B changes, recompute the sum. That works for two inputs and one output.
It does not survive a real application. A real app has dozens of inputs, intermediate computations that depend on each other (cleaning depends on the raw data, the model depends on the cleaning, three tables depend on the model), and outputs that depend on those. Wire it by hand and every time you add or rename an input you must hunt down every handler that touches it and edit them all. You will miss one.
Reactivity inverts the responsibility. Instead of saying “when A changes, recompute the sum”, you declare “the sum is whatever A plus B currently is”, and the system works out when and how to recompute it. To do that, it builds a graph of who depends on whom and pushes invalidation signals through it.

title: 8  Reactivity
text:
NoteAnalogy: a spreadsheet



A spreadsheet is the purest reactive system most people have used. You write =A1+B1 in a cell. You never write “when A1 changes, update this cell”. The spreadsheet knows the dependency because it read your formula, and it updates the cell whenever A1 or B1 changes. Shiny is a spreadsheet for arbitrary R computations, and the rest of this chapter is how the spreadsheet knows.

title: 8  Reactivity
text:
8.2 The three kinds of node
Every node in a reactive graph plays one of three roles.
Sources are the roots. They bring values in from outside the reactive system. The familiar one is input$something, carrying values from the browser. Others include reactiveVal(), which holds a mutable value you set from code, and reactivePoll(), which checks an external resource on a timer.
Conductors are interior nodes. A reactive() is a conductor: it depends on other nodes and can be depended on in turn. A conductor caches its last computed value and recomputes only when that cache has been invalidated. This is the memoise closure from Chapter 3, now with automatic invalidation.
Endpoints are the leaves. They exist for their side effects. The two kinds are observe(), which runs purely for effect, and output$something assignments, whose effect is to update the browser.

title: 8  Reactivity
8  Reactivity
text:
8.4 Stopping propagation: isolate()
isolate() reads a reactive value without registering a dependency. It is the equivalent of event.stopPropagation() in the browser. Use it to consume a current value without being notified of future changes.
observe({
  trigger   <- input$go_button       # depend on the button
  current_x <- isolate(input$x)      # read x WITHOUT depending on it
  cat("Snapshot of x at button press:", current_x, "\n")
})
This is the canonical “take a snapshot when X happens” pattern. The convenience wrappers eventReactive() and observeEvent() are sugar over exactly this combination of depending on one thing and isolating another.

title: 8  Reactivity
text:
8.5 The flush cycle
Shiny does not push invalidations one at a time. They accumulate, and at the next flush of the session’s event loop, all invalidated reactives are recomputed together, in dependency order. This guarantees that even when several inputs change in the same instant, each downstream node recomputes exactly once, from a consistent set of inputs, and no half-updated intermediate state leaks to the user. It is the same idea as batched re-rendering in modern web frameworks: collect the changes, then settle the whole graph once.

title: 8  Reactivity
text:
8.6 Two principles for Shiny architecture
Two design rules fall straight out of the graph picture.
Push computation toward conductors. If three outputs each need a cleaned version of the data, do not clean it inside all three. Make one reactive() that cleans, and have all three outputs depend on it. Otherwise the cleaning runs three times per change, because each output body evaluates independently. Conductors exist to deduplicate shared work, which is the same benefit memoisation gave you in Chapter 3.
Use endpoints for side effects only. An observe() that does heavy computation stalls the flush cycle for the whole session, because the flush is not done until the observer finishes. If you find an observer doing real work, that work almost always belongs in a reactive(), with the observer reduced to consuming the result.

title: 8  Reactivity
text:
8.7 The relationship to targets
A pipeline built with targets is, in a real sense, the reactive graph idea applied to long-running computation. Each target is a node. Dependencies are inferred from unevaluated code, exactly the move from Chapter 4. When an upstream input changes, downstream targets are invalidated and recomputed in dependency order. The only real difference is the timescale and where the cache lives. Shiny settles its graph within a session in milliseconds, with the cache in memory. targets settles its graph across runs, with the cache on disk, persisting between sessions. Same shape, different clock. We make that explicit in the next chapter.

title: 8  Reactivity
text:
8.8 What you can now predict

Why a Shiny output updates without you ever writing the wiring that connects it to its inputs.
Why a shared computation should live in one reactive() rather than being repeated in several outputs.
Why a heavy observe() can make an entire app feel frozen.
Why targets and Shiny feel so similar once you ignore the difference in timescale.

title: 8  Reactivity
text:
8.9 Exercises

Draw the reactive graph for an app with two numericInputs, one reactive() that adds them, one renderText that shows the sum, and one observeEvent on a button that snapshots the current sum and prints it. Label every node as source, conductor, or endpoint.
In a small app, deliberately introduce the “three consumers, one expensive computation repeated three times” anti-pattern, with cat() tracing. Then refactor the computation into a single reactive() and confirm from the trace that it now runs once per change instead of three times.
Write a tiny reactive system in base R, no Shiny. Provide make_signal(initial), returning something you can get and set, and watch(signal, callback), which runs callback whenever the signal is set. Use only closures and environments. You will not reproduce Shiny’s flush cycle, but you will reproduce the read-is-subscribing core.
Explain, in terms of dependency registration, why wrapping a value in isolate() inside a reactive() can stop that reactive from updating when you expected it to. Construct a small example where this is a bug and a small example where it is exactly what you want.

title: 9  Performance
text:
9.1 Copy-on-modify
R has a reputation for being slow. The truth is more useful: R is slow when you fight its memory model and fast when you work with it, and the difference can be a factor of thousands on the very same calculation. This chapter cashes in the foundations from Chapter 1. Copy-on-modify is pointers and reference counts. Vectorisation is the cache and the CPU loop. The three layers of caching in the R world are the one idea from Chapter 3 at three timescales.
R appears to copy a value whenever you assign it to a new name. Physically, it usually does not, and the reason is the pointer picture from Chapter 1. The runtime keeps, for each value, a count of how many names point to it. As long as only one name points to a value, R can modify it in place. The instant a second name points to the same value, the next attempt to modify it through either name forces a real copy first, so the other name is left untouched.
This is exactly the locker-and-sticky-note from Chapter 1. y <- x copied the note, not the locker. Only when you tried to change the contents through y did R duplicate the locker so that x would still see the original. The reference count is how R knows whether duplication is necessary: one pointer means safe to edit in place, more than one means copy first.
The sharp practical consequence is the most common performance mistake in R: growing a vector in a loop.
The bad version is slow not because R is interpreted but because c(x, i^2) keeps creating a new, longer vector and, since x still points at the old one, copying everything across each time. Pre-allocating means there is a single value with a single pointer, so each x[i] <- ... edits it in place with no copy.

title: 9  Performance
text:
library(lobstr)

x <- 1:1e7
obj_addr(x)              # the address of the value on the heap

y <- x                   # no data is copied; y points at the same value
obj_addr(y)              # identical address to x

y[1] <- 99               # NOW the copy happens, to protect x
obj_addr(y)              # a new address
obj_addr(x)              # unchanged


# Bad: each iteration may copy the whole vector so far.
x <- c()
for (i in 1:10000) x <- c(x, i^2)        # roughly n-squared work

# Good: allocate once, then fill in place.
x <- numeric(10000)
for (i in 1:10000) x[i] <- i^2           # linear work

# Best: no R-level loop at all.
x <- (1:10000)^2                          # one operation, run in C

title: 9  Performance
text:
9.2 Why vectorisation is fast
When you write x + y on two numeric vectors, R does not run a loop in the interpreter. It calls into compiled C code that walks the two arrays in tight machine instructions, the CPU loop from Chapter 1, and the cost of the R-level interpretation (parsing the expression, dispatching +) is paid once for the whole vector rather than once per element.
There is a second, deeper reason, and it is the cache. An R numeric vector is stored as its elements laid out contiguously in memory. When the CPU processes it, every expensive trip to RAM pulls in a whole cache line of 64 bytes, eight neighbouring doubles, all of which are about to be used. The hardware’s bet from Chapter 1 pays off on every line. A pointer-chasing alternative, where each number sits in a separate object somewhere on the heap, loses that bet on every access and spends most of its time waiting on memory.
So the well-worn advice to “vectorise” is really two wins stacked together: you pay the interpreter’s overhead once instead of a million times, and you let the cache work for you instead of against you.






ImportantKey idea: a rule of thumb you can now justify



If you can read a function’s body by printing it, it runs in the interpreter and will be slow in a tight loop. If its body is .Primitive(...), .Internal(...), or .Call(...), it runs in C and will be fast over a whole vector. Vectorising means handing the inner loop to C and laying your data out contiguously so the cache cooperates. Both halves come straight from Chapter 1.


`+`
# function (e1, e2)  .Primitive("+")        run entirely in C

sum
# function (..., na.rm = FALSE)  .Primitive("sum")

mean
# function (x, ...)  UseMethod("mean")      dispatches, then C

title: 9  Performance
text:
9.3 Three layers of memoisation
Caching shows up at three natural scales in the R world, and they are the same idea, a cache plus an invalidation rule, applied at growing scope. You met the engine in Chapter 3; here are its three homes.
Function-call memoisation, via memoise::memoise or your eight-line version. The cache is an environment captured in a closure, living in memory. It lasts only for the current R session. Use it for a slow pure function called repeatedly with the same arguments, which is common in Shiny when an expensive result depends on a small set of selectable inputs.
Reactive caching, via shiny::reactive and shiny::bindCache. The cache lives in the reactive node, and invalidation is automatic through the reactive graph of Chapter 7. It is scoped to a session, or to the whole application with bindCache(cache = "app").
Pipeline memoisation, via targets. The cache lives on disk, keyed by a hash of the target’s command, its dependencies, and its inputs. It survives across R sessions, across machines if you share the store, and across time. Invalidation is automatic through the pipeline graph.






NoteOne idea, three clocks



In-closure cache, in-session and gone when R exits. Reactive cache, settled within a session in milliseconds. Pipeline cache, on disk and good for months. The art is matching the layer to the lifetime of the work. Caching a one-millisecond function on disk wastes more time on input-output than it saves; trying to use an in-memory cache for a six-hour model fit means recomputing it every time R restarts.

title: 9  Performance
text:
9.4 Concurrency: future and promises
R is single-threaded: one CPU loop, one thing at a time. To run work in parallel across cores or machines, or simply off to the side so a Shiny session stays responsive, use the future framework.
library(future)
library(promises)

plan(multisession)             # run futures in background R processes

f <- future({
  Sys.sleep(3)
  "done"
})

# The current session is free to keep working here.
value(f)                        # blocks only when you finally need the result
Inside Shiny, the promises package provides a then-style operator, %...>%, so an output can be declared as “render the result of this future when it finishes, without blocking the session in the meantime”.
output$plot <- renderPlot({
  future({ slow_computation(input$x) }) %...>% plot()
})
A word of warning about vocabulary. The word promise here is not the same as the promise from Chapter 4. There it meant the unevaluated-expression mechanism behind lazy evaluation. Here it means a value that will be ready later, the concurrency sense familiar from JavaScript. R reuses the word for two different ideas. Context disambiguates, but the collision is real, so name your variables with care.

title: 9  Performance
text:
9.5 Three rules for Shiny performance
Everything above condenses into three operational rules.
Pre-allocate. Never grow a vector inside a reactive block. If you are, you have almost certainly missed a vectorised primitive that would do the whole thing at once.
Push heavy CPU off the main thread. Anything taking more than about a tenth of a second belongs in a future, so the session stays responsive. Fill the visual gap with a spinner from shinycssloaders.
Cache at the right layer. Per-session transient results go in reactives. Results shared across sessions go in bindCache(cache = "app"). Results that must survive a restart go in memoise with a disk backend, or as a targets stage if they are part of a pipeline.

title: 9  Performance
text:
9.6 What you can now predict

Why y <- x is instant even for a huge x, and exactly when the hidden copy finally happens.
Why growing a vector in a loop is quadratic, and why pre-allocation fixes it.
Why a vectorised operation beats an R loop twice over, in interpreter overhead and in cache behaviour.
Which of the three caching layers a given computation belongs in, from how long its result needs to live.

title: 9  Performance
text:
9.7 Exercises

Use lobstr::obj_addr and tracemem() to demonstrate copy-on-modify in three cases: assigning a vector to a new name, then modifying one element of the new vector, then modifying an element via data.table’s := operator instead of base assignment. Explain each address change, or lack of one, using reference counts.
Time the three vector-building approaches (c() in a loop, pre-allocate then fill, and the fully vectorised form) for a vector of length one million. Plot the three timings and explain the ranking from this chapter.
Take a genuinely slow pure function, a bootstrap with many resamples is ideal, and wrap it in memoise::memoise() with a filesystem backend. Re-run it from a fresh R session and confirm the second call is instant. Which of the three caching layers did you just use, and why was a disk backend necessary?
In a small Shiny app, make a slow plot render asynchronously with future and promises, and confirm the rest of the app stays responsive while it computes. Then explain, using the single-threaded model, what would have happened without the future.

title: 10  Metaprogramming: Code as Data
text:
10.1 Capturing code without running it
In Chapter 4 we saw something quietly radical. When you write filter(df, x > 3), R does not run x > 3 and pass the answer. It hands the function the unevaluated code and lets the function decide what to do with it. That was our first glimpse of an idea that runs all the way through R: code is data. A piece of R code is an ordinary object that you can store in a variable, take apart, build up piece by piece, and choose to run later, or not at all.
This chapter makes that idea explicit and hands you the small toolkit R provides for working with code as data. We will take it slowly, one tool at a time, because the next chapter uses these tools to do something that sounds impossible and turns out to be short: build a working interpreter for a small language, in R itself.
The most basic move is to grab a piece of code and hold it, unevaluated. The tool is quote().
Nothing was added. e is not 3. It is the expression 1 + 2, sitting in a variable, waiting. You can ask what kind of object it is, and you can run it later with eval().
So quote() captures, and eval() runs. Between those two moments, the code is just an object you can examine and change like any other. That gap, between capturing code and running it, is the entire playground of metaprogramming.

title: 10  Metaprogramming: Code as Data
text:
e <- quote(1 + 2)
e
#> 1 + 2

class(e)
#> [1] "call"

eval(e)
#> [1] 3







NoteAnalogy: a recipe card versus a cooked meal



quote(1 + 2) is a recipe card that reads “add 1 and 2”. It is not the meal. You can read the card, copy it, scribble on it, file it away, or hand it to someone else. eval() is the act of actually cooking from the card. Most programming hands you meals; metaprogramming lets you handle the cards.

title: 10  Metaprogramming: Code as Data
text:
10.2 Code is a tree
Once you are holding a piece of code, you can look inside it, because an R expression has a simple, regular structure: it is a tree. The technical name is an abstract syntax tree, but the idea is plain. Take this expression:
e <- quote(f(x, y + 1))
A call like this behaves like a list. Its first element is the function being called, and the rest are the arguments.
e[[1]]            #> f        (the function)
e[[2]]            #> x        (first argument)
e[[3]]            #> y + 1    (second argument, itself a smaller call)

as.list(e)
#> [[1]] f
#> [[2]] x
#> [[3]] y + 1
Notice that the second argument, y + 1, is itself a call with its own pieces. Expressions nest inside expressions, which is exactly what makes them a tree.
Now the reveal that ties this to the rest of the book. In R, everything that happens is a function call, including the things that do not look like function calls. Arithmetic is a function call. So is an if, an index with [, even a block of code in curly braces. You can write them in their hidden call form:
`+`(1, 2)              #> 3
`if`(TRUE, "yes", "no")  #> "yes"
`[`(c(10, 20, 30), 2)    #> 20
The backticks just let you refer to these operators by their real names, which are ordinary functions. This uniformity is a gift inherited from Lisp: every expression is one of only three things. It is a constant (like 1 or "hello"), a name (like x, a reference to a variable), or a call (a function applied to some argument expressions). That is the whole grammar. Because the grammar is so small and regular, code is genuinely easy to treat as data, and in the next chapter we will lean on exactly these three cases.

title: 10  Metaprogramming: Code as Data
10  Metaprogramming: Code as Data
text:
10.4 Building code from scratch
Just as you can take code apart, you can put it together. The base tool is bquote(), which quotes an expression but lets you splice values in using .().
v <- 5
bquote(x + .(v))
#> x + 5
The x stayed as code, but .(v) was replaced by the current value of v. You can also assemble a call directly from its parts:
call("sum", 1, 2, 3)
#> sum(1, 2, 3)
This matters because it lets a program generate code tailored to its inputs: a function that, given a column name, builds the right summary expression, or a report generator that assembles analysis code from a configuration.

title: 10  Metaprogramming: Code as Data
text:
10.5 Running code needs an environment
We met eval() above, but there is a second ingredient we glossed over. To run a piece of code, you need two things: the code itself, and a place to look up the names in it. That place is an environment, exactly the side-table from Chapter 2.
e <- quote(x + 1)

eval(e)
#> Error: object 'x' not found     (no 'x' in scope here)

eval(e, list2env(list(x = 10)))
#> [1] 11                          (now 'x' is 10, in the environment we gave)
The same expression gives an error or the answer 11, depending only on the environment you evaluate it in. This is the deepest point of the chapter, and it is worth stating plainly.






ImportantKey idea: running code is an expression plus an environment



Code on its own is inert. To run it you must pair it with an environment that gives meaning to its names. Expression plus environment is the irreducible recipe for evaluation. Hold this pair in mind: it is literally the two arguments to eval(), and in the next chapter it becomes the two arguments to the evaluator we build ourselves.

title: 10  Metaprogramming: Code as Data
text:
10.6 Quasiquotation: the modern toolkit
The rlang package, which powers the tidyverse, wraps these ideas in a tidier interface called quasiquotation. You capture code with expr() and splice values or other code back in with !! (pronounced “bang-bang”).
library(rlang)

v <- 5
expr(x + !!v)
#> x + 5
This is the same move as bquote’s .(), just with friendlier names. And it closes a loop from Chapter 4. When you write a tidyverse helper with { col }, you are using a shortcut for “capture the caller’s unevaluated expression with enquo, then splice it back in with !!”. Now you can see all the way down: { } is metaprogramming with a comfortable handle on it.

title: 10  Metaprogramming: Code as Data
text:
10.7 A statistician’s daily metaprogramming: formulas
You already use code as data, probably without naming it, every time you fit a model. A formula like y ~ x + z is not computed when you write it. It is a captured, unevaluated expression that also remembers the environment it was written in.
f <- y ~ x + z
class(f)
#> [1] "formula"

f[[1]]            #> `~`
f[[2]]            #> y
f[[3]]            #> x + z
A formula is a tree, just like the expressions above, with ~ as its operator. This is why lm(y ~ x, data = d) can look up y and x as columns of d: the formula handed lm the unevaluated code, and lm chose where to evaluate it, exactly as filter did in Chapter 4. Every modelling function in R is doing metaprogramming on your behalf.

title: 10  Metaprogramming: Code as Data
section: 10.8 The boundary is thin: data made of functions
text:
10.8 The boundary is thin: data made of functions
Here is a small demonstration that is worth a few minutes of wonder, because it shows the code-and-data duality from the other side. We have seen that code can be treated as data. It is also true that data can be built out of nothing but functions. Suppose R had no way to pair two values together. We could invent the pair using only closures from Chapter 3.
cons <- function(a, b) function(selector) selector(a, b)
car  <- function(pair) pair(function(a, b) a)
cdr  <- function(pair) pair(function(a, b) b)

p <- cons(1, 2)
car(p)   #> 1
cdr(p)   #> 2
There is no list and no vector here. A “pair” is a closure that has captured two values and waits to be told which one to hand back. car and cdr ask for the first or the second. You would never build pairs this way in real R, but the exercise reveals something deep: the line between a piece of data and a piece of code is much thinner than it looks. The next chapter walks straight across that line.






TipWhy this matters for your work



Once you can see metaprogramming, the tidyverse stops being a collection of special cases. dplyr verbs, ggplot2‘s aes(), data.table’s j argument, model formulas, and the way targets reads your pipeline (Chapter 4) are all the same move: capture code, inspect or transform it, and evaluate it in a chosen environment. Understanding the move lets you read these libraries’ source with comprehension, debug their surprising error messages, and write your own small tools that take code as input.

title: 10  Metaprogramming: Code as Data
text:
10.9 What you can now predict

Why the same expression can error in one place and succeed in another, with nothing changed but the environment.
Why lm(y ~ x, data = d) can find y and x in a data frame even though they are not variables in your console.
Why the tidyverse can let you name columns without quotes, and what { } is shorthand for.
How a tool could read your code and answer questions about it without running it.

title: 10  Metaprogramming: Code as Data
text:
10.10 Exercises

Use quote() to capture mean(x) + sd(y). Without running it, extract just the two function names being called. Then evaluate the whole expression in an environment where x and y are vectors you supply.
Extend count_adds into count_calls(expr, fname) that counts how many times any given function is called inside an expression. Test it by counting the * operations in quote(a * b + c * d * e).
Write a function make_power(n) that builds and returns the code for raising its argument to the power n, using bquote. For example make_power(3) should return the expression x ^ 3. Then evaluate it for a specific x.
Confirm that a formula carries its environment. Create y ~ x inside a function where a local variable secret exists, return the formula, and from outside the function recover secret through the formula’s environment. Explain the result using Chapter 2.

title: 11  The Metacircular Evaluator
text:
11.1 The plan: eval and apply
This is the chapter the whole book has been walking toward. Its title sounds forbidding, so let us defuse it immediately. A metacircular evaluator is a program that runs a language, written in that same language. We are going to write a small interpreter for a useful slice of R, in R. When it works, you will have built, with your own hands, a tiny version of the very thing that has been running your code all along.
The reason this is the climax of a book called Think Like the R Runtime is simple. There is no surer way to understand a runtime than to build one. Every idea from the earlier chapters will reappear here, not as a topic but as a line of code we cannot do without: environments become the place we look up names, closures become the way our functions remember where they were born, and the expression-plus-environment recipe from Chapter 9 becomes the signature of our main function.
We will build it in small, safe steps, testing after each one. Do not skim. Type each piece into an R session and watch it work, because the understanding is in the watching.
Running code turns out to be a conversation between two functions that call each other.
To evaluate an expression, you look at what kind of expression it is. If it is a number, it is its own value. If it is a name, you look it up. If it is a call, you evaluate the function and each of its arguments, and then you apply the function to those argument values.
To apply a function to some arguments, you create a fresh environment, bind the arguments to the function’s parameter names in it, and then evaluate the function’s body in that new environment.
Read those two paragraphs again and notice that each mentions the other. Evaluate calls apply; apply calls evaluate. This mutual recursion is the engine of every interpreter ever written. Everything else is detail.
We need just two helpers first, for our environments. We will not reinvent them: we will use R’s own environments from Chapter 2, which already do everything we need, including the parent chain.
env_get uses inherits = TRUE, which means: if the name is not in this environment, walk the parent chain and keep looking. That one flag is lexical scoping, handed to us for free.

title: 11  The Metacircular Evaluator
text:
env_new <- function(parent) new.env(parent = parent)
env_get <- function(env, name) get(name, envir = env, inherits = TRUE)
env_set <- function(env, name, value) assign(name, value, envir = env)

title: 11  The Metacircular Evaluator
11  The Metacircular Evaluator
11  The Metacircular Evaluator
11  The Metacircular Evaluator
section: 11.5 Step 4: functions of our own, with closures

title: 11  The Metacircular Evaluator
text:
11.6 Why it is called metacircular
Our evaluator is written in R, and it evaluates R. The language doing the interpreting and the language being interpreted are the same. That circularity is the point of the name, and it is also a genuine intellectual loophole worth being honest about: we did not have to explain how + adds, or how do.call calls, because we borrowed those directly from the R underneath us. A metacircular evaluator explains a language in terms of itself, which is illuminating about structure (how scoping, closures, and special forms fit together) while quietly leaning on the host language for the very bottom. To explain that bottom you would descend to Chapter 1: the C code, the registers, the memory. The two ends of this book meet here.

title: 11  The Metacircular Evaluator
text:
11.7 The real thing
You have been using a more powerful version of lang_eval and lang_apply all along. R exposes them directly. eval() is the real evaluator, and quote() (Chapter 9) is how you hand it code, and an environment is where it runs.
eval(quote(2 * (3 + 4)))   #> 14
When you call eval, R does, in fast and battle-tested C, what our lang_eval did in R: it dispatches on the kind of expression, looks names up by walking environments, treats if and function and a few others as special forms, and applies functions by making a new evaluation environment parented at each function’s definition environment. You now know what is inside that call, because you wrote a small one yourself.

title: 11  The Metacircular Evaluator
text:
11.8 What you can now predict

Why interpreting a language is a conversation between an “evaluate” step and an “apply” step.
Why some constructs (if, function, and in R quote itself) cannot be ordinary functions and must be special forms.
Why closures and lexical scoping require nothing more than parenting a call’s environment at the function’s definition environment.
What R’s own eval is doing, in outline, every time it runs a line of your code.

title: 11  The Metacircular Evaluator
text:
11.9 Exercises

Add multiplication-free subtraction’s friend: extend global_env with a new primitive, for example %% for remainder, and confirm your evaluator can use it with no change to lang_eval. Why did no change to the evaluator’s code turn out to be necessary?
Add a let special form so that let(x, 5, x + x) evaluates the third argument in a new environment where x is bound to 5. Where in lang_eval does it belong, and why must it be a special form rather than an ordinary function?
Our evaluator is eager: it evaluates every argument before applying. Make one argument lazy. Change lang_apply so that instead of receiving evaluated arguments it receives the unevaluated argument expressions plus the calling environment, and evaluates each only when first needed. You will have reinvented the promise from Chapter 4.
Add an error if a closure is called with the wrong number of arguments. Then explain, in terms of lang_apply, why R’s own “unused argument” and “missing argument” errors exist and where in an interpreter they must be detected.

title: 12  Capstone: One System, Every Idea
text:
12.1 The scenario
Every idea in this book exists, in some form, inside any non-trivial R system. To show that they are not ten separate topics but one connected way of thinking, this final chapter walks through a single realistic system and points to where each idea lives. We will not build the whole thing line by line; we will trace the machinery.
Picture a small application built for an agricultural statistics office. It reads administrative data on holdings and yields, fits a small-area estimate of mean output for each region, caches the fits, and serves the results through a Shiny dashboard that lets a user filter regions, drill into individual estimates, and download the underlying data. It is the kind of system a working statistician actually ships, and it touches every chapter.
crumbs: 12  Capstone: One System, Every Idea

title: 12  Capstone: One System, Every Idea
text:
12.2 Where each idea lives

12.2.1 Foundations (Chapter 1)
The data is large, so memory layout is not an afterthought. The yield series are held as plain numeric vectors, contiguous in memory, so that the per-region summaries run as vectorised operations that the cache cooperates with rather than as pointer-chasing loops. The decision to keep the heavy reference tables in one shared structure, loaded once, rather than rebuilt per request, is a direct application of “moving data is the expensive part”.


12.2.2 Environments (Chapter 2)
Helper functions live in an R/ directory and are sourced into a clean environment by _targets.R. The pipeline’s definition environment therefore contains only those helpers, with no scratch objects and no test data, so nothing irrelevant is dragged into a target’s dependency footprint. Scope discipline here is a performance and correctness decision, not a stylistic one.


12.2.3 Closures (Chapter 3)
The fitting routine is a function factory. make_estimator(prior, model_spec) returns a function whose only argument is the data, with the prior and the specification captured in its environment. Swapping priors or specifications is then a one-line change, and the data-flow code never has to mention configuration at all.


12.2.4 Lazy evaluation (Chapter 4)
The pipeline is declared, not run. tar_target(estimate, fit_per_region(clean, model_spec)) captures an unevaluated expression. targets reads that expression, discovers that estimate depends on clean and model_spec, and adds the edges to its graph, all before any fitting happens. The plan exists because the code could be inspected as data.


12.2.5 The call stack and conditions (Chapter 5)
Each per-region fit is wrapped in withCallingHandlers(). A region that fails to converge is logged with its diagnostic and replaced by a sentinel value, while the remaining regions continue. The pipeline finishes successfully and carries a record of which regions failed and why. A single bad region is a local event, not a fatal one, because we chose a calling handler over an unwinding one.


12.2.6 Object systems (Chapter 6)
Each estimate is an S3 object: a list with class nuts_estimate, holding the posterior summary, the relevant data subset, and metadata. print gives a one-line view, summary the full posterior table, plot a forest plot of regional estimates with intervals. R6 is reserved for the one component that manages a resource over time: the connector that authenticates once against the source database and is reused across the run.


12.2.7 Reactivity (Chapter 7)
The dashboard loads the pipeline’s output once when a session starts. A single reactive() filters the estimates to the regions the user has selected. Three downstream outputs, a table, a map, and a forest plot, all depend on that one conductor, so the filter runs once per change rather than three times. The graph deduplicates the shared work automatically.


12.2.8 Performance (Chapter 8)
The pipeline is memoised on disk by targets: only regions whose underlying data changed are refitted when the pipeline is rerun. Inside the dashboard, the map render is slow because it depends on a large shapefile, so it runs inside a future and the session stays responsive while it draws. The shapefile is loaded once at startup, not per session, the same “load shared data once” decision as in the foundations.


12.2.9 Metaprogramming (Chapter 9)
Code-as-data is not only inside targets; it surfaces in the analysis itself. The models are specified with formulas, output ~ region + year, which are captured, unevaluated expressions that the fitting code evaluates against each region’s data. A small internal helper builds those formulas programmatically from a configuration file, so that adding a covariate is a change to data, not to code. When the dashboard lets an analyst type a custom filter, that text is parsed into an expression, checked by walking its tree to forbid anything dangerous, and only then evaluated in a controlled environment. Every one of these is the capture-inspect-evaluate move from Chapter 9.


12.2.10 Building a runtime (Chapter 10)
The evaluator chapter does not ship inside this system, and it is not meant to. Its role is what it leaves behind in the person who built it. The engineer who has written lang_eval reads the targets source and recognises the dependency walk as an expression traversal. They read a confusing tidyverse error and locate it as a failure in the capture-and-evaluate step. They reason about a Shiny reactive’s scope without hesitation, because they have parented an environment at a definition environment with their own hands. The interpreter is the rung at the top of the ladder: having built one, the reader sees every other piece of machinery in this system as a variation on something they understand from the inside.

title: 12  Capstone: One System, Every Idea
text:
12.3 What you have learned
Read that list again and notice that the ideas are not really separable. The factory in Chapter 3 only works because of the lexical scoping in Chapter 2, which only makes sense given the heap in Chapter 1. The pipeline graph in Chapter 4 is invalidated by the same kind of machinery as the reactive graph in Chapter 7. The on-disk cache in Chapter 8 is the in-closure cache from Chapter 3, grown up and moved to disk. The reference semantics that make R6 useful in Chapter 6 are the same reference semantics that make environments the one exception to copy-on-modify in Chapter 2. The metaprogramming of Chapter 9 is the lazy evaluation of Chapter 4 made deliberate, and the evaluator of Chapter 10 is all of it at once, assembled into a small machine that you built. It is one fabric.
The argument that opened this book, borrowed from the course that inspired it, was that you cannot reason about a language by memorising rules: you have to be able to predict what the runtime will do. Everything here has been in service of that, for R. You now have a working model that runs from electrical switches up to the reactive graph, and a habit to go with it.






TipThe habit



When R surprises you, do not look up the rule. Predict, from the model, what should happen, then run a tiny experiment to check. When the prediction and the experiment disagree, you have found a gap in the model, and closing it is how the model gets sharper. Read the source of a function you have always taken for granted. Print an object you have always just used. Ask why, and answer with mechanism. That habit, more than any single fact in this book, is what makes the difference.

title: 12  Capstone: One System, Every Idea
text:
12.4 Where to go next
Three books go deeper than this course, and you are now ready for them. Hadley Wickham’s Advanced R covers environments, functional programming, and metaprogramming in full depth, and is the natural next step. Norman Matloff’s The Art of R Programming is excellent on performance and debugging from a systems perspective. For Shiny in production, Engineering Production-Grade Shiny Apps by Colin Fay and colleagues builds on the reactive model from Chapter 7. For pipelines, the targets manual by Will Landau is thorough and free online.
Beyond reading, the best next step is to take a piece of R you rely on, a package you use, a Shiny app you maintain, a pipeline you run, and explain it to yourself using the model in this book. Find the environments. Find the closures. Find the graph. Find the cache. When you can do that for code you did not write, the model is yours.
